speaker: interviewer
text: --two. Interview with Mr. Henry Blasenstein. Mr. Blasenstein, you were-- you were mentioning one particular incident where three Polish citizens were hung by the Germans right outside your apartment. One thing I just wanted to clarify, in Bochnia, was there actually a ghetto set up in Bochnia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when did that take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Oh, by the-- around the end of 1940 or beginning '41. I think it the in 1940, if I'm not mistaken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family's place where you live, was that inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was like almost inside. In other words, the front-- the front entrance went into the marketplace. But there was a back entrance too that went into the ghetto. So we could stay in the apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ghetto physically sealed off?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you couldn't-- you couldn't get out where they announced that this is the ghetto. It comprises this and this streets. And if they would catch you, they will shot you or arrest you, you know, because you were wearing armbands, you know, or your-- on your arm with a Star of David in the middle of [INAUDIBLE] Judah, mean Jew. So you were afraid to-- to go behind that. There were no barriers or-- or chains, nothing. It was open. But nobody would-- would dare to-- to go out there because not only you were afraid for the German, but you were afraid for the Poles too. They were not better from the Germans. So if they would see you, they may report you to the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were Poles able to come in to the ghetto area if they wanted to, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't-- I don't think so. No, they were not allowed to, because they didn't want to let them in, because they would do all kind of business with the Jews, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you had mentioned the armbands that you had to wear. Were there other restrictions or-- or laws passed against Jews in Bochnia at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the restriction where that there was a certain time where you couldn't go out on the street anymore in the evening. I don't remember what time it was. And you had to report to the same where you had to report to for work. And you had to only buy in Jewish stores. And other restriction where you were afraid to walk too much on the street because the SS men may just came in and-- and-- and arrest some-- some people, you know, and you would never see them anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: This happened to people you knew, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. It happened to people we knew-- I knew, yeah. Disappear, you know? You-- you never know what's happened to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about food? Were you able to obtain food in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, you could if you had money. You could buy it, naturally. Yeah. There were Jewish stores, and you could-- you could buy food, yeah. There were restriction on it. There many time, you know, you had to stay in lines to get the food. And if you were in a long line, by the time you got to-- to the store, there wasn't any-- anything-- anymore left, bread or-- or milk, or whatever you wanted to buy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the-- the family business? How was that affected when the Germans came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as before, before the-- they closed the ghetto, the business was still open. In other words, we-- we still had the business, you know? But once the-- they closed the ghetto, they send the Jews to the ghetto, we had to abandon everything in the house, in the-- in the-- in the yard, and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just lost-- the family just lost the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, sure. Not only our-- my family, every Jew who ever had any businesses.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to keep money, have money to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I imagine they still-- they have some money, you know, accumulated from before, you know? See, they-- they-- most of the Jewish stores, even before the ghetto, they closed down. See, there was closed by-- by us in the [GERMAN], a big like a supermarket. At that time, there was no supermarket. So they closed that store, I think, maybe a week or two weeks after the German came in. And they put in a sign on the store. What I going to say it in Polish. [INAUDIBLE] you're going to translate. [SPEAKING POLISH] That mean, here were in that store was a-- a man who was cheating the Poles, and he was sent out to that town, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: That was like Dachau before the war in Germany, you know? You know there was that was a camp before the war for-- for-- for people. And-- and that's what they did with the Jewish businesses. How long did-- did you stay in the ghetto in Bochnia, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- I was sent still when there was the-- the ghetto in Bochnia. I was sent to work in a town by the name Klaj. That was by-- 15 kilometers from Bochnia. And we used to travel there by train every morning, early in the morning, and came back in the evening. And that-- that was like a forest. But the Polish army had there a munitions there. And we were working there. In other words, they used to came trains with ammunition. And we used to sort them from caliber, you know, the sizes. And down used to come German trucks. And we used to load the trucks. And they used to go that time already to the Russians front.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you receive any-- were you fed while you were doing this at all, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We-- we didn't get nothing. We had to take our-- our lunch from home. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they pay you at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they did not pay. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- there was in the beginning. Before I used to go there, they-- we used to work for-- for Germans. They used to pay us something. But after that, they didn't pay.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when the ghetto was formed in-- in Bochnia, what effect did that have, for example, on the synagogues and on Jewish religious observance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] manage what to do. A lot-- a lot of people were afraid to go to the synagogues, because they could, you know, during the services, you know, come in and-- and-- and take out some people, and send them away, but nobody knows where. Right away, there were-- there were two or three-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] three times where they send away a lot of the Jews from the ghetto. In other words, the ghetto was smaller and smaller and smaller, less and less people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe one of those times.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, by that time when they started to liquidate, little by little, the ghettos, I was already in Klaj. In that place, they built barracks from us. And we used to stay there overnight. But they used to-- you see, that Judenrat, you know, they're what the-- the-- the-- the people who are the-- the heads of the-- of the ghetto, they got orders from the German, by tomorrow, you have to bring in 10,000 Jews or 5,000 or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they had to-- to-- to bring that people to a certain place. And they sent them away. So there was 10,000 people less in the ghetto, because in the ghetto, was a lot of people, a lot of Jews, because they were brought, you know, from the surrounding vicinities, you know? They were small towns, a lot of small towns around Bochnia. And they all were brought into the ghetto in Bochnia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every time after they send away in the ghetto was smaller and smaller, in other words, less and less people. And I remember even when my mother and my sister, she was sent out, I think, with the third one. And she went to Belzec. And my uncle still left in the ghetto. He was still in the ghetto because he was working for the-- for the Germans in a fur business. So the Germany needed fur, you know, for the soldiers. And I was that time already in Klaj. But when it came the last-- last what they called an [GERMAN], you know-- in German, they call it [GERMAN], last transport-- that time they liquidated completely the-- the ghetto. There was no more ghetto. And by that time, they even sent us away from Klaj, and we went to a-- to a camp, but by name Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when-- when the ghetto-- you say that your mother and your sister left on the third?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately. I think it was the third time when they were, you know, cutting the ghetto, sending away people. It was smaller and smaller.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were at Klaj, were you able to communicate with your mother at all, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the beginning, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] oh, no. See, we-- we could come once every two weeks, we could come back to Bochnia for a weekend. But by that time, when they allowed us to come, my mother wasn't there anymore. She was gone. She was sent away already.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when I got to Bochnia, the apartment was empty. Nobody was home. So I remember after that, I didn't want-- want to anymore come. So I remember, I stayed with my uncle that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: In his apartment, he had one room. He was staying with seven, eight people. So they did not even-- didn't even have room for me where to sleep, you know? So after that, I didn't-- you could still come to Bochnia, but I didn't want to come anymore because there was nobody there, and it was nobody to come to. You mentioned that people from the surrounding areas were all Jews were sent into the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Bochnia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother like forced to share an-- the apartment with other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: With strangers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had-- we had one room and a kitchen. And in the kitchen, there was another family living, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: In other words, we had the bedroom, and they had the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know them previously, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were people from-- from another town. They went not even from the surrounding area. They were far, far away in-- from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't have no choice. They didn't-- you couldn't choose whom you want to have there. But to leave it here, the Judenrat, they-- they assigned people that you have to go live here and here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that difficult, living with [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, it was difficult, you know? First of all, you had-- we had to cross through the kitchen where I used to live. And, you know, you do not have no privacy, you know? We lived-- me, and my mother, and sister lived in the one room. And it was a hardship, naturally.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that eventually, barracks were set up at-- at Klaj. Were you asked to build those barracks, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't build them. It's possible-- I think the barracks were still from the Poles, from the Polish army. No, there was three barracks. And that's where we-- we stay in this barracks. No, we didn't build them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the barracks like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, barracks, there was-- there was one-- one bed on top of the other one, you know? How you call that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a bunk bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bunk bed, yes. Right. And one barrack could be 60 people, maybe more. And we got there, there was a kitchen. We got a-- during lunch time, they gave us a soup, and in the morning, a coffee with a piece of bread. And-- and that's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that enough food, or-- or do you remember being hungry there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't enough, no. I was always hungry. That was my biggest problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hunger, ah, it's very hard to describe what means hunger. Hunger is a terrible thing. But what could you do? If you got a piece of bread, you-- that you had to have it for a whole day. So you just grabbed a piece of it. And you say the rest is for later.

speaker: interviewer
text: But later, you took another piece because you couldn't [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] control yourself, you know? So it came in the morning. You didn't have nothing because you-- you ate it up, you know? You had to wait till-- till the following day till you got another piece of bread. And especially, when you are that time 17 or 18-- 18 years old, and you're tall, and skinny, and you need to-- you need nourishment, you know? Was there any--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was only in 1940. By the end of '41, that wasn't-- the bad time didn't come yet. The worst time came after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned always being hungry while at-- at Klaj. Were there any incidences that you can recall where-- where workers were stealing food from each other, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, sure. If you had-- had left a piece of bread, you put it under your hat. So in case somebody wants to steal it, you can feel it, you know? Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else do you remember about working at Klaj, living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. After when we were living in the barracks, I-- we were there just only, I think, a few months. Because down it came the last-- the last transport was sent out from Bochnia. They liquidated the ghetto in Bochnia. So after liquidated the ghetto in Bochnia, they send us away from Klaj too. We are working there, and physically, it wasn't so bad in-- in-- in-- Klaj, you know? There was nobody shot or killed, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: After your-- your mother and your sister were deported from the ghetto, did you hear any rumors or-- or did you attempt to contact them in any way? Or what happened with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't even know where they were sent away. We only knew that that was-- they were sent-- deported. But you didn't know where they were deported. I think I didn't find that out till after the war where they went.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about-- how about your uncle? Where-- where was he at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he was still in Bochnia. But I don't know what happened to him. I have no idea. I imagine they were sent to Belzec, too-- to the last transport that was-- was deported, I think, to Belzec, too. But I have-- couldn't help. But no confirmation, and there was nobody left from the family there that you could ask or find out or could who would know anything about it, because I was left alone, you know? I had no family, though.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were-- while you were at Klaj, was it at all possible to obtain extra food? Was there any way to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some people had some connection with the-- in the-- with the people in the kitchen could get some extra food, naturally, could get an extra-- extra plate of soup, you know? But I did not have connection, you know? I didn't know the people, you know? So I couldn't get extra food. And I remember one time, I got sick there being in Klaj. And the ghetto was still-- there was still a ghetto in Bochnia. And I got the-- the jaundice. Jaundice is it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] I was all yellow, you know? So they send me-- they send me back to Bochnia to a hospital. But I went to Bochnia, and I didn't want to go to the hospital, because if I would go to the hospital, I wouldn't be here anymore. So I remember I stayed with my uncle. He was still in the ghetto that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stayed with him for three weeks. And I remember I couldn't eat nothing. I didn't no medication, no nothing, because I was afraid to go to the hospital to get medication. And then after three weeks, I felt better, and I went back to Klaj. The hospital that you're talking about, was that inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you so afraid of the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because a lot of people were killed in the hospitals, you know? They didn't help you. They didn't treat you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you say that people were killed, were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I mean not killed by shotting or something. They didn't shot them. But they didn't treat them, so they die, you know, from the sickness, whatever they had it, you know, because they didn't medicate you, you know? There was medication for-- for jaundice, you know, or other things. But they didn't give you that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe for me the-- the final-- when you finally left Klaj. When you-- When the ghetto was liquidated, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew when that day when the ghetto was liquidated because the rumors, you know, it's not for [INAUDIBLE]. And I remember the day when they said today that the ghetto is being liquidated, and everybody is being sent away, deported. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that day, even the German soldiers that was that time, there was no-- no SS that time. There was Wehrmacht, regular German soldiers. They were very nice. I can't complain against them. They-- they knew about that too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they didn't take care if you did work or didn't work, you know? They were sympathetic to you. But after that, a few days later, we're getting ready to go-- to go to work, because the barrack filled like fume that feed from-- from the place where we worked, you know? Suddenly, We see trucks arriving in front of the barracks. We saw something is brewing, something. We didn't know what's going on. And suddenly, they tell you, everybody out and on the trucks. And that's when I took us to-- to Plaszow. Now, were you able to-- to take any possessions with you, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, you could take-- you could take everything you had with you. But the minute you arrived in Plaszow, you have to take out everything, and they took away everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were at-- at Klaj, was-- was there a particular-- did you have like a friend there who-- who you were with or someone who you spent a lot of time with or got to know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm, not really. Later on in-- when I was other camp, I-- I had a close friend who are living in Israel. But from-- from Klaj, I don't remember, though.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these trucks came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came and they took us to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a few hours. I wasn't so far. It was like from Klaj to-- Plaszow was like distance from-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] from Bochnia to Cracow was 40 kilometers. And that was suburbs of-- of Cracow. So it could be another 5 kilometer. It wasn't so far. It took maybe a couple hours. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: On-- when we got-- the first thing when we got into Plaszow, naturally, we had to-- to-- to leave it on the floor, everything we had with us. They-- they allow you still to take some-- some personal things, they still allow you to take. But most importantly, [INAUDIBLE] you have money or jewelry, you know, and if you say no, and they looked, and they-- they shot you on the spot, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But right-- the same-- the first day when we came into Plaszow, so they didn't took us to barracks. They took us what they call it an appeals place, in other words, like the a-- a assembly place. And there were already thousands of people assembled there. And when we coming there, they were hanging three people, three young boys where they were hung that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, we found out the reason they were hung up because they were whistling a Russian song. That is a very popular song, 'Volga Volga,' [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH]. That means Volga is the Russian, you know, river.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's why they-- they hung them. Because, you see, the-- the guards in Plaszow were Ukrainian, but they were many time worse than Germans. And they understand Russian. And that's-- that was the first day, our reception in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And down they sent us in barracks. And the following day, they-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they make groups, you know, for-- for 10 or 15 people and were sending us to work, you know? And when we-- we arrived in-- in Plaszow, that must have be [INAUDIBLE] end '42 of beginning '43. I-- could be beginning of '43. Wintertime?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: This was winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was springtime. But I can't remember springtime, because that time, they deported all the Jews from the Krakau ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to end tape two here. We'll pick this up with tape number three.
